title: A Likeness
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some people hang portraits up
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In a room where they dine or sup:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the wife clinks tea-things under,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And her cousin, he stirs his cup,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Asks ן¿½Who was the lady, I wonder?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½T is a daub John bought at a sale,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Quoth the wife, looks black as thunder:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What a shade beneath her nose!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Snuff-taking, I suppose,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Adds the cousin, while Johnן¿½s corns ail.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or else, there ן¿½s no wife in the case,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the portrait ן¿½s queen of the place,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Alone mid the other spoils
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of youth, masks, gloves and foils,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And pipe-sticks, rose, cherry-tree, jasmine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the long whip, the tandem-lasher,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the cast from a fist (ן¿½not, alas! mine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But my masterן¿½s, the Tipton Slasherן¿½),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the cards where pistol-balls mark ace,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a satin shoe used for cigar-case,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the chamois-horns (ן¿½shot in the Chablaisן¿½)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And prints Rarey drumming on Cruiser,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Sayers, our champion, the bruiser,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the little edition of Rabelais:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where a friend, with both hands in his pockets,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ May saunter up close to examine it,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And remark a good deal of Jane Lamb in it,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But the eyes are half out of their sockets;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That hair ן¿½s not so bad, where the gloss is,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But theyן¿½ve made the girlן¿½s nose a proboscis:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Jane Lamb, that we danced with at Vichy!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What, is not she Jane? Then, who is she?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All that I own is a print,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ An etching, a mezzotint;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½T is a study, a fancy, a fiction,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet a fact (take my conviction)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Because it has more than a hint
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of a certain face, I never
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saw elsewhere touch or trace of
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In women I ן¿½ve seen the face of:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just an etching, and, so far, clever.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I keep my prints, an imbroglio,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fifty in one portfolio.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When somebody tries my claret,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We turn round chairs to the fire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Chirp over days in a garret,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Chuckle oן¿½er increase of salary,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Taste the good fruits of our leisure,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Talk about pencil and lyre,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the National Portrait Gallery:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then I exhibit my treasure.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ After we ן¿½ve turned over twenty,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the debt of wonder my crony owes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is paid to my Marc Antonios,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He stops me ן¿½Festina lentן¿½!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Whatן¿½s that sweet thing there, the etching?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How my waistcoat-strings want stretching,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How my cheeks grow red as tomatos,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How my heart leaps ! But hearts, after leaps, ache.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½By the by, you must take, for a keepsake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That other, you praised, of Volpatoן¿½s.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The fool! would he try a flight further and say
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He never saw, never before to-day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What was able to take his breath away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A face to lose youth for, to occupy age
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the dream of, meet death with, why, Iן¿½ll not engage
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But that, half in a rapture and half in a rage,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I should toss him the thingן¿½s self ן¿½ן¿½T is only a duplicate,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A thing of no value! Take it, I supplicate!ן¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Browning represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Robert Browning's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
